meta.title: CCA-F-3: Reliable Agent Design, MCP, and Claude Code

questions.stem:
A product team is debating whether every user request should go through a multi-agent workflow with planning, tool use, and review steps.

When is the simpler design usually better?

options.text: When the task is straightforward and can be answered directly without tools or multi-step control flow
When the team wants to maximize orchestration overhead
When every request must be decomposed even if it is trivial
When latency and cost are irrelevant to the user experience

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Anthropic guidance recommends reserving agentic patterns for tasks that genuinely need them.

**B** is incorrect because extra orchestration is a cost, not a benefit by itself.

**C** is incorrect because forced decomposition adds needless complexity.

**D** is incorrect because user experience still matters in production systems.

questions.stem:
A team processes 6,000 customer documents overnight using the standard synchronous Claude API. Each request costs an average of $0.10. They switch to the Message Batches API for the same workload.

What is the expected cost for 6,000 documents using the batch API?

options.text: $600 — the Message Batches API uses the same per-token pricing as the synchronous API
$0 — the Message Batches API is free for non-interactive, overnight workloads
$300 — the Message Batches API applies a 50% discount on input and output tokens, reducing 6,000 × $0.10 from $600 to $300
$150 — the discount scales with batch size; batches over 5,000 requests receive a 75% discount

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because the Message Batches API does not use synchronous pricing — it applies a 50% discount on both input and output token costs. This discount is the primary economic incentive for batching independent requests.

**B** is incorrect because the Message Batches API is not free. It is priced at 50% of the standard per-token rate — a meaningful discount, but not zero cost.

**C** is correct because the Message Batches API charges 50% of the standard synchronous input and output token rates. For a workload that costs $0.10 per request at synchronous pricing, the batch price is $0.05 per request — reducing the total from $600 to $300 for 6,000 documents.

**D** is incorrect because the batch discount is a flat 50%, not a tiered rate that scales with batch size. There is no volume tier that provides a 75% discount.

[Message Batches - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/message-batches)

questions.stem:
An agent is given the task: 'Delete all log files older than 30 days.' Before executing, it finds 8,000 log files matching the criteria. Some may still be needed by active processes.

Which action reflects correct agent behaviour before proceeding?

options.text: Delete all 8,000 files immediately — the task is explicit
Delete a random sample of 100 files first to test the operation
Pause, report the count and any ambiguities to the user, and request confirmation before mass deletion
Skip the deletion and suggest the user do it manually to avoid risk

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because mass deletion of 8,000 files without confirmation is an irreversible action with potential for significant unintended damage.

**B** is incorrect because sampling 100 random deletions does not confirm the operation is safe, and still causes irreversible changes without user awareness.

**C** is correct because mass irreversible actions require explicit user confirmation before execution. The agent should surface the count, note potential risks (active processes), and wait for approval — this is the human-in-the-loop principle for high-impact actions.

**D** is incorrect because refusing to act on a valid task is unhelpful. The correct response is to confirm scope, not refuse entirely.

[Building effective agents - Anthropic](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/building-effective-agents)

questions.stem:
A team needs to generate SEO-optimized titles for 80,000 product descriptions. The task is not time-critical — results are needed within 24 hours. Each product description is independent.

Which API approach is most cost-effective for this workload?

options.text: Send all 80,000 descriptions in a single large Messages API call for bulk efficiency
Use the Messages API synchronously, processing one description per call, as fast as possible
Use the Message Batches API, which processes large volumes of independent requests asynchronously at a 50% cost discount
Use streaming responses so results arrive as soon as each title is generated

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because the Messages API has per-request context limits — concatenating 80,000 descriptions into one call is not feasible, and single-call bulk discounts are not offered.

**B** is incorrect because 80,000 synchronous calls runs sequentially and at full input pricing, which is both slow and more expensive than the batch alternative.

**C** is correct because the Message Batches API is designed for exactly this pattern: high-volume, independent, non-time-critical requests processed asynchronously. It offers 50% discounts on input and output token costs, making it the most cost-effective option for overnight batch workloads.

**D** is incorrect because streaming delivers tokens as they are generated but does not reduce cost or enable asynchronous batch processing at scale.

[Message Batches API - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/message-batches)

questions.stem:
A system has independent subtasks: classify the request, retrieve policy context, and draft a response. None of those subtasks depends on the others finishing first.

Which orchestration choice is BEST?

options.text: Run the independent subtasks in parallel and combine the results later
Force every subtask to run strictly one after another
Let one generalist agent guess all outputs without any decomposition
Delay all work until a human completes every subtask manually

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because parallel fan-out is efficient when subtasks are independent.

**B** is incorrect because serial execution adds latency without benefit here.

**C** is incorrect because skipping decomposition loses the advantages of specialization.

**D** is incorrect because manual blocking is unnecessary for independent work.

questions.stem:
A team runs multiple Claude subagents on the same task and compares their outputs. One subagent is assigned to find counterarguments to the proposed solution. Another verifies data accuracy.

This multi-agent structure is best described as which pattern?

options.text: Sequential pipeline — each agent depends on the previous agent's output
Speculative execution — agents generate alternative solutions for comparison
Adversarial checking — specialised subagents critique or audit the primary agent's work from different angles
Recursive decomposition — each agent breaks its subtask into smaller subtasks

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because sequential pipelines run in series with dependency chains; the described agents run independently on the same task.

**B** is incorrect because speculative execution generates multiple candidate solutions and picks the best; here agents play different roles (counterarguments, accuracy) rather than all solving the same problem.

**C** is correct because the counterargument agent and accuracy-verification agent act as specialised critics. This adversarial or multi-perspective checking pattern intentionally introduces different viewpoints to stress-test the primary solution.

**D** is incorrect because recursive decomposition breaks a problem into sub-problems — the agents here are not decomposing the task recursively.

[Building effective agents - Anthropic](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/building-effective-agents)

questions.stem:
A financial risk team builds a credit assessment pipeline. Claude must reason through multiple interacting factors — borrower history, macroeconomic indicators, collateral quality, and regulatory constraints — before producing a risk rating. Standard prompting produces shallow analysis that misses interactions between factors. The team wants Claude's intermediate reasoning steps to appear in the API response as a separate, inspectable block.

Which API feature supports this?

options.text: Increase `max_tokens` to give Claude more room to explain its reasoning in the final response
Enable extended thinking with `thinking: {type: "enabled", budget_tokens: N}` — Claude produces a separate `thinking` block before the final response, containing its intermediate reasoning steps
Set `temperature: 0` to make Claude's analysis more deterministic and complete
Add 'think step by step' to the system prompt — Claude will include reasoning in the visible response

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because `max_tokens` controls the maximum length of Claude's output response. Increasing it does not create a structurally separate reasoning block — it only allows a longer final answer.

**B** is correct because extended thinking allocates a configurable `budget_tokens` budget for Claude to reason privately. The output includes a dedicated `thinking` content block (separate from the final `text` block) that contains Claude's intermediate reasoning steps. This block is inspectable by the calling application and is structurally distinct from the final answer.

**C** is incorrect because `temperature: 0` makes token selection deterministic — it does not produce a separate reasoning block or change the depth of analysis. It reduces variation but does not enable extended reasoning.

**D** is incorrect because chain-of-thought instructions in the system prompt embed reasoning in the final response text — there is no structurally separate block, and the reasoning cannot be cleanly isolated or given an explicit token budget. Extended thinking must be explicitly enabled to produce an inspectable reasoning block.

[Extended thinking - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/extended-thinking)

questions.stem:
A team is evaluating whether to build a multi-agent system or a single-agent system for a task that:
- Requires 4 specialist capabilities
- Has a 10-second latency budget
- Can be fully described in 2,000 tokens
- Has no irreversible actions

Which factor most strongly argues FOR the single-agent approach?

options.text: The task requires 4 specialist capabilities, suggesting each needs its own agent
The 10-second latency budget may not tolerate the coordination overhead of multiple agents
The lack of irreversible actions means a multi-agent system is safe to use
The 2,000-token description fits in a single prompt, making one agent efficient enough

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because 4 capabilities doesn't automatically require 4 agents — a single well-prompted agent can handle multiple capabilities if they don't require specialist depth.

**B** is a valid consideration but coordination overhead is measured in seconds, not necessarily violating a 10-second budget.

**C** is incorrect because the absence of irreversible actions is relevant to safety, not to the architecture choice.

**D** is correct because if the full task can be specified in 2,000 tokens, a single agent call is highly efficient. Multi-agent overhead (coordination, context passing, latency) is hard to justify when the task fits comfortably within a single context window. Simplicity should be the default.

[Building effective agents - Anthropic](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/building-effective-agents)

questions.stem:
A developer is writing the system prompt for an orchestrator agent that coordinates three specialist subagents: a web researcher, a data analyst, and a summariser. The orchestrator must assign work, aggregate results, and handle cases where a subagent returns an error.

Which system prompt element is MOST critical for the orchestrator to function reliably?

options.text: A clear description of the orchestrator's role, the identity and capability of each subagent, the required output format for the final synthesis, and explicit instructions for handling subagent failures or partial results
A statement telling the orchestrator to 'use best judgment' without specific role constraints
The full source code implementation of each specialist subagent
An instruction to ignore subagent failures and always produce a confident final response

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because an effective orchestrator system prompt must define: (1) the orchestrator's role and boundaries so it doesn't attempt specialist work itself; (2) each subagent's capability so the orchestrator assigns tasks correctly; (3) the expected output format so synthesis is structured and parseable; and (4) failure-handling instructions so the pipeline degrades gracefully rather than producing incomplete or fabricated results. Each element directly maps to prompt engineering best practice for complex multi-step tasks.

**B** is incorrect because 'best judgment' without constraints leaves the orchestrator's behaviour undefined. Without explicit role, capability, and failure-handling instructions, the orchestrator will produce inconsistent results across runs.

**C** is incorrect because source code in a system prompt does not give Claude the capability to execute it — Claude cannot run code from its system prompt. The prompt should describe subagent capabilities in natural language, not implementation details.

**D** is incorrect because ignoring subagent failures leads to fabricated final responses. Explicit failure handling — retry, escalation, or graceful degradation — is essential for production robustness.

[Prompt engineering overview - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/prompt-engineering/overview)

questions.stem:
An orchestrator dispatches a data-analysis task to a subagent. The subagent returns: 'Analysis complete. Key findings: revenue increased 12%, costs decreased 3%.'

The orchestrator needs to include these figures in a final report. What validation step should the orchestrator perform before using the figures?

options.text: No validation needed — Claude subagents do not hallucinate numbers
Ask the subagent to confirm the figures by regenerating the answer a second time
Cross-check the reported figures against the underlying data source the subagent had access to
Accept the figures and add a disclaimer to the report that numbers are unverified

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because all language models including Claude can produce numerical hallucinations, especially when summarising data.

**B** is incorrect because asking Claude to regenerate the same answer is likely to produce the same (possibly incorrect) numbers — regeneration does not constitute independent verification.

**C** is correct because the only reliable validation of a numerical claim is to check it against the source data. If the subagent had access to a database or dataset, the orchestrator should verify the stated percentages match the actual records before publishing them in a report.

**D** is incorrect because adding a disclaimer is inadequate — it shifts the verification burden to the reader rather than addressing the accuracy problem.

[Building effective agents - Anthropic](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/building-effective-agents)

questions.stem:
A team wants to run their Claude-powered document processing pipeline as part of a GitHub Actions CI workflow. The pipeline calls Claude's API and two external data APIs. All three require secret keys.

Which approach correctly and securely provides these secrets to the GitHub Actions workflow?

options.text: Commit the secret keys directly in the workflow YAML file so the CI runner can read them
Store each key as an encrypted GitHub Actions secret, then reference them as environment variables in the workflow steps — secrets are never exposed in logs or workflow files
Pass the secret keys as plain-text in the git commit message that triggers the workflow
Use a public endpoint for each API that requires no authentication — avoiding credentials in CI entirely

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because committing secret keys in a workflow YAML file exposes them in version control history. Anyone with repository access — current or future — can read the keys, even after they appear to have been removed from the latest commit.

**B** is correct because GitHub Actions Secrets store values encrypted at rest and inject them only into the specific workflow runs where they are referenced. They do not appear in workflow YAML files, are masked in log output, and are only accessible to workflows with repository write access.

**C** is incorrect because commit messages are stored permanently in git history and are visible to anyone who can clone or view the repository. Using commit messages as a secrets transport is a critical security vulnerability.

**D** is incorrect because using unauthenticated public endpoints is not a viable solution for the Claude API or most external data APIs — they require authentication. Public endpoints would expose the pipeline to rate limiting, data mixing, and production data risks.

[Claude Code GitHub Actions](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/github-actions)

questions.stem:
A content moderation pipeline uses Claude to classify posts into one of three categories: `REMOVE`, `REVIEW`, `APPROVE`. On re-testing, 14% of posts receive a different classification when submitted again with identical inputs. The team's target is under 3% reclassification rate.

Which approach most directly reduces classification inconsistency?

options.text: Increase `max_tokens` to give Claude more room to reason about each classification
Increase temperature to explore a wider range of classification options per post
Remove the category definitions from the prompt so Claude applies its own interpretation

Lower temperature, define the three permitted output values explicitly in the system prompt with decision criteria for each, and add a validation loop that retries any response not matching the three allowed values

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because `max_tokens` controls response length, not output consistency. A longer response does not reduce the probability of Claude selecting a different label on re-run.

**B** is incorrect because higher temperature increases stochastic variation in token selection — it makes outputs more random, not less. This would worsen the reclassification rate, not improve it.

**C** is incorrect because removing category definitions removes the constraints that anchor Claude's classification decisions. Without explicit categories, output variation increases.

**D** is correct because lower temperature reduces stochastic variation; explicitly defining the three permitted values and their decision criteria narrows the output space and removes ambiguity; and a validation-and-retry loop catches any non-conforming response before it reaches downstream systems. Together these three controls directly address the sources of classification inconsistency.

[Increase consistency - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/test-and-evaluate/strengthen-guardrails/increase-consistency)

questions.stem:
An automated financial report generator occasionally produces sections with missing numeric values shown as 'N/A'. Downstream aggregation systems expect all report fields to contain numeric values — they crash on 'N/A' strings.

Which system prompt instruction most directly prevents this?

options.text: Add 'try your best to fill in all fields' to the system prompt
Increase max_tokens so Claude has room to calculate missing values
Tell Claude to skip sections entirely when it cannot find the data
Explicitly instruct Claude in the system prompt: 'If a numeric field value is unavailable, substitute 0 and append a footnote explaining the substitution — never output N/A or leave any field empty'

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because 'try your best' is vague — it does not specify what Claude should output when data is missing. Without an explicit substitution rule, Claude may still produce 'N/A' when it cannot determine the correct value.

**B** is incorrect because `max_tokens` controls output length, not Claude's ability to compute or fill in missing data values. More response tokens do not resolve the underlying data unavailability.

**C** is incorrect because skipping sections entirely produces reports with missing fields — downstream systems would still crash, on a missing key rather than an 'N/A' value.

**D** is correct because the system prompt is the authoritative place for output format constraints. Specifying exactly what to substitute (0) and what annotation to include (a footnote) when data is unavailable gives Claude a concrete, unambiguous rule to follow — eliminating the conditions that produce 'N/A' outputs.

[System prompts - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/prompt-engineering/system-prompts)

questions.stem:
A team builds a pipeline where Agent A generates a report and Agent B posts it to an external publishing platform. If Agent B fails due to a network error, the report is lost and must be regenerated from scratch.

Which design change prevents this data-loss failure mode?

options.text: Retry Agent A first whenever Agent B fails — regenerating the report ensures fresh content
Checkpoint Agent A's output to durable storage before Agent B attempts to publish — the report persists across Agent B failures and the pipeline can resume without regenerating it
Combine Agents A and B into a single agent so there is no inter-agent data handoff that can fail
Use a higher temperature on Agent B to make it more resilient to transient network errors

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because retrying Agent A regenerates the report from scratch on every Agent B failure. This wastes compute, increases latency, and introduces inconsistency if Agent A's output is not deterministic across runs.

**B** is correct because checkpointing Agent A's output to durable storage before handing off to Agent B is a fundamental pipeline resilience pattern. If Agent B fails, the persisted report can be reused for retry or recovery without re-running Agent A. This is a standard checkpointing approach in agent pipeline design: each stage writes its result to persistent state before passing control to the next stage.

**C** is incorrect because merging agents removes the modularity that makes the pipeline maintainable and testable. It also does not resolve the data-loss problem — a merged agent that fails mid-generation still loses intermediate state.

**D** is incorrect because temperature controls the randomness of Claude's token sampling during text generation. It has no effect on network reliability or Agent B's ability to handle transient connection failures.

[Building effective agents - Anthropic](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/building-effective-agents)

questions.stem:
A team wants Claude to return machine-readable data with stable field names so downstream code can parse it safely.

Which prompt design is MOST helpful?

options.text: Define the required structure clearly and validate the output before using it
Ask for any format and hope the parser adapts
Rely only on a vague persona with no formatting instruction
Maximize sampling diversity so field names change less

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because clear structure plus validation reduces downstream parsing failures.

**B** is incorrect because unconstrained outputs are brittle for parsers.

**C** is incorrect because persona alone does not guarantee shape.

**D** is incorrect because sampling diversity does not enforce structure.

questions.stem:
A team writes a system prompt for a document formatting service. The prompt says: 'Format documents professionally.' After deployment, Claude produces inconsistent heading levels — sometimes H1, sometimes H2 for the same type of section.

What is the most direct fix?

options.text: Add 'Be consistent with heading levels' to the system prompt
Increase max_tokens so Claude has more space to reason about formatting
Provide explicit heading rules: 'Use H1 for document title, H2 for major sections, H3 for subsections' with one formatted example
Lower temperature to reduce variation in heading selection

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because 'be consistent' is vague — consistent with what? Claude needs concrete rules, not a meta-instruction.

**B** is incorrect because heading selection is not a reasoning problem requiring more space; it is a formatting rule that needs to be specified.

**C** is correct because explicit, specific heading rules with a concrete example give Claude the exact information it needs to produce consistent output. 'H1 for title, H2 for major sections, H3 for subsections' is unambiguous.

**D** is incorrect because temperature 0 selects the most probable token but does not guarantee adherence to unspecified formatting rules.

[System prompts - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/prompt-engineering/system-prompts)

questions.stem:
A code review assistant needs to output feedback in a specific format: one line per issue, starting with the file path, colon, line number, colon, then the issue description. Claude sometimes omits the line number or uses different separators.

Which prompt technique most reliably enforces this format?

options.text: Tell Claude 'Always use the exact format specified'
Show Claude 3-5 example outputs in the exact required format before the task description
Describe the format in natural language: 'File, then colon, then line, then colon, then issue'
Use temperature 0 to force Claude to follow the most common output pattern it has seen

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because 'always use the exact format' is an instruction without a definition — Claude cannot follow a format it has only been told to use 'exactly'.

**B** is correct because few-shot format examples are the most reliable way to enforce an output format. Seeing 3-5 examples of `src/auth.py:42: Missing null check for user_id` makes the pattern concrete and learnable.

**C** is incorrect because natural language description of format is ambiguous. 'Colon' between 'file' and 'line' — is that one colon or two? Examples eliminate this ambiguity.

**D** is incorrect because temperature 0 selects the most probable token but does not guarantee adherence to a specific format the model hasn't seen before.

[Prompt engineering overview - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/prompt-engineering/overview)

questions.stem:
A system prompt for a customer-facing chatbot spans 4,000 tokens and includes persona instructions, product knowledge, tone guidelines, and prohibited topics. A developer adds a new constraint to restrict discussion of pricing. Despite the addition, Claude still answers pricing questions.

What is the most likely cause?

options.text: The new constraint was added at the end of the system prompt, but end-of-prompt instructions have lower priority
Claude ignores constraints that come after a certain token count threshold

The 4,000-token prompt contains many positive framing statements (e.g., 'Claude can help with...') that implicitly suggest pricing is in-scope; the single constraint is overwhelmed by the existing framing
Pricing constraints can only be enforced via the API's moderation endpoint, not via the system prompt

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because there is no documented priority hierarchy based on position in the system prompt. All sections can influence Claude's behaviour.

**B** is incorrect because Claude reads the full system prompt regardless of length (within context limits).

**C** is correct because a single negative constraint ('do not discuss pricing') can be overwhelmed by extensive positive framing that implies pricing is a valid help topic. The fix is to reinforce the constraint with an explicit example: 'If a user asks about pricing, respond: I cannot provide pricing information — please visit our website.'

**D** is incorrect because system prompt constraints are the standard mechanism for topic restrictions; a separate moderation endpoint is not required.

[System prompts - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/prompt-engineering/system-prompts)

questions.stem:
A Claude integration exposes several tools, but the model often chooses the wrong one because the descriptions overlap and the input contracts are vague.

Which change BEST improves tool selection accuracy?

options.text: Write precise tool descriptions and input schemas so each tool’s purpose is unambiguous
Add more nearly identical tools to increase coverage
Remove parameter details so Claude can infer them
Rename the tools randomly between runs

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because tool descriptions and schemas strongly influence correct invocation.

**B** is incorrect because overlapping tools increase confusion.

**C** is incorrect because hiding parameters weakens the contract.

**D** is incorrect because random naming hurts reliability.

questions.stem:
A developer defines an MCP tool with this description: 'Gets data.' Claude frequently fails to call this tool when it should and calls it when it shouldn't.

What is the most effective fix?

options.text: Rename the tool to include keywords matching Claude's training data
Rewrite the description to specify exactly what data it retrieves, under what conditions it should be called, and what it returns
Add more tools to the server so Claude has more choices and learns which to prefer
Use a forced tool_choice call to always invoke this tool

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because tool selection is based on the description's meaning, not keyword matching to training data.

**B** is correct because a vague description like 'Gets data' gives Claude insufficient information to decide when the tool is appropriate. A specific description — 'Retrieves the current inventory count for a given product SKU from the warehouse database. Call when the user asks about stock levels or availability.' — enables correct selection.

**C** is incorrect because adding more tools makes selection harder, not easier. Fewer, well-described tools are better.

**D** is incorrect because always forcing this tool call means it executes even when inappropriate, which is worse than the current behaviour.

[MCP: Tools](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/docs/concepts/tools)

questions.stem:
An MCP tool accepts an optional `format` parameter with supported values: `json`, `csv`, `html`. If omitted, the server defaults to `json`. The tool description does not mention this parameter.

What problem does this omission cause?

options.text: No problem — optional parameters do not need to be documented
Claude will always pass the `format` parameter with a random value, causing errors
Claude cannot use the optional parameter to tailor output format because it does not know the parameter exists or what values it accepts
Claude will error if the server returns JSON when Claude expected a different format

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because optional parameters should be documented — Claude can only use features it knows about.

**B** is incorrect because Claude will not pass unknown parameters; it will simply omit them.

**C** is correct because tool descriptions are the only mechanism Claude has for understanding what parameters a tool accepts. Without documentation of the `format` parameter, Claude will always receive JSON even when the task would benefit from CSV or HTML output.

**D** is incorrect because Claude processes whatever the server returns; it does not enforce an expected format based on unspecified parameters.

[MCP: Tools](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/docs/concepts/tools)

questions.stem:
An MCP server provides five standard database operations as tools. A developer wants to offer Claude a pre-written prompt template for generating a database migration report — including a header, list of changes, and rollback instructions.

Which MCP primitive should the developer use to deliver this template?

options.text: MCP Tool — define a `generate_migration_report` tool that returns the template text
MCP Resource — expose the template as a readable document with a stable URI
MCP Prompt — define a reusable prompt template that injects the migration report structure into the conversation
System prompt — embed the template in the application's system prompt directly

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because a Tool invokes a function with side effects — it is for actions, not for injecting structured instruction templates.

**B** is incorrect because Resources are addressable, read-only data. They are fetched when needed but are not designed to inject prompt content into the conversation flow at invocation.

**C** is correct because MCP Prompts are reusable instruction templates. When invoked, they inject structured content — such as a migration report header, change list, and rollback instructions — directly into the conversation. This is the designed use case for MCP Prompts.

**D** is incorrect because embedding the template in the application's system prompt couples it to the application code and makes it unavailable through the MCP server's standardised interface.

[MCP: Prompts](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/docs/concepts/prompts)

questions.stem:
An MCP deployment includes a tool that changes production configuration.

Which practice is MOST appropriate?

options.text: Make the side effects explicit and apply safeguards before allowing the tool to run
Describe the tool as read-only so the model calls it more often
Hide errors from the model to avoid interruptions
Merge all tools into one broad command with no schema

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because side-effectful tools need clear labeling and stronger controls.

**B** is incorrect because mislabeling dangerous tools increases risk.

**C** is incorrect because concealing failures prevents recovery.

**D** is incorrect because over-broad tools reduce safe routing and validation.

questions.stem:
An MCP server exposes internal company documentation as resources. The documentation is frequently updated. Claude fetches a resource and provides advice based on it. The user reports the advice is based on outdated content.

What MCP mechanism should the server use to keep Claude's view of the resource current?

options.text: Push the updated document to Claude's system prompt after every update
Implement resource subscriptions: when a resource updates, the server notifies the client so Claude can re-fetch the current version
Add a timestamp to every resource URI so Claude requests a new URL when content changes
Rebuild the MCP server from scratch whenever documentation is updated

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because pushing to the system prompt requires external coordination outside the MCP protocol and is not a scalable approach for frequently updated resources.

**B** is correct because MCP resources support subscriptions: when a subscribed resource changes, the server sends a notification to the client. Claude can listen for these notifications and re-fetch the resource to get current content.

**C** is incorrect because timestamp-based URIs require the client to periodically poll with new URLs — this is a workaround, not the designed mechanism.

**D** is incorrect because rebuilding the entire server for documentation updates is operationally impractical.

[MCP: Getting started](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/docs/concepts/resources)

questions.stem:
A new developer joins a team using an MCP server with 25 tools. The developer asks: 'How do I know what each tool does and when to use it?' Claude is the primary consumer of these tools.

Where should this usage guidance live?

options.text: In a separate README file that developers read before using the MCP server
In the tool descriptions and parameter descriptions within the MCP server definition — so Claude reads it automatically
In the team wiki, linked from the README
In the system prompt of every application that uses the MCP server

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because a README helps the developer but does not help Claude, which cannot read a README file.

**B** is correct because tool descriptions in the MCP server definition are the primary source of guidance for Claude. Every parameter, use case, and constraint should be documented there — this is what Claude reads to determine when and how to call each tool.

**C** is incorrect because team wiki documentation helps human developers but is not accessible to Claude during tool selection.

**D** is incorrect because adding tool guidance to every application's system prompt is redundant and fragile — it must be updated in multiple places whenever the tool changes.

[MCP: Tools](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/docs/concepts/tools)

questions.stem:
Two MCP servers expose similarly named capabilities, and the model sometimes picks the wrong one.

What is the BEST mitigation?

options.text: Disambiguate names and descriptions so each tool has a clearly distinct purpose
Keep the collisions because the model will learn by intuition alone
Remove all descriptions from both servers
Encourage the model to call both tools every time

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because clear differentiation reduces accidental tool collisions.

**B** is incorrect because ambiguity remains without clearer contracts.

**C** is incorrect because removing descriptions worsens selection quality.

**D** is incorrect because always calling both tools adds cost and side-effect risk.

questions.stem:
An MCP tool `archive_document` takes a `document_id` string. Claude sometimes calls it with an integer instead of a string (e.g., `12345` instead of `"12345"`). The server crashes on integer input.

Which fix is most robust?

options.text: Tell Claude in the tool description to 'always pass document_id as a string'
Define `document_id` as `type: string` in the JSON schema — this prevents Claude from passing an integer and the MCP framework rejects malformed calls
Rebuild the server to handle both string and integer inputs
Add a validator at the system prompt level: 'Before calling archive_document, confirm document_id is a string'

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because text instructions to 'pass as a string' reduce but do not eliminate type errors — Claude may still infer an integer.

**B** is correct because declaring `type: string` in the JSON schema is machine-enforced: Claude is trained to conform to the schema, and MCP frameworks validate parameters against the schema before the tool executes. Type-level enforcement is more reliable than text instructions.

**C** is incorrect because accepting both types makes the server more permissive rather than making the interface correct. This treats a symptom rather than the root cause.

**D** is incorrect because system-prompt validation instructions add overhead and are not reliably enforced.

[MCP: Tools](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/docs/concepts/tools)

questions.stem:
A team has two MCP tools: `search_kb` (searches a knowledge base) and `search_db` (queries a relational database). Claude frequently calls the wrong tool because both descriptions currently read: 'Searches for information.'

Which MCP tool schema change most directly resolves the confusion?

options.text: Rename `search_kb` to `knowledge_base_search` and `search_db` to `database_query` to differentiate by name alone

Rewrite each tool's `description` field to be explicitly contrastive: `search_kb` — 'Searches unstructured documentation and free-text articles; use for conceptual or definition questions'; `search_db` — 'Queries structured relational tables with SQL; use for filtered or aggregated data lookups'
Merge the two tools into a single `search` tool that routes internally based on the query type
Add a fallback instruction: if one tool returns no results, automatically call the other

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because renaming alone does not tell Claude when to use each tool. Names that are more distinctive help marginally, but without contrastive descriptions Claude still cannot reliably infer from the name alone which tool applies to a given query.

**B** is correct because the `description` field in an MCP tool schema is the primary mechanism through which Claude decides when to invoke a tool. Writing contrastive descriptions — explicitly stating what each tool is for and when to prefer it over the other — directly encodes the routing logic in the tool schema. Claude reads these descriptions to match a query to the most appropriate tool.

**C** is incorrect because merging the tools delegates routing to server-side logic, removing Claude's ability to make an informed choice. It also reduces transparency — the orchestrating model cannot see or reason about the tool selection.

**D** is incorrect because adding a fallback instruction is a mitigation for failed calls, not a fix for the root cause. Calling the wrong tool first and falling back to the correct one wastes latency and may cause unintended side effects.

[MCP: Tools](https://modelcontextprotocol.io/docs/concepts/tools)

questions.stem:
A long-running assistant repeatedly loses the most important constraints after many turns.

Which approach is MOST likely to help?

options.text: Summarize and retain the critical state instead of keeping every past token verbatim
Keep appending all history forever without prioritization
Reset the conversation after every user turn
Remove all constraints once the conversation becomes long

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because compaction strategies preserve the most important information within context limits.

**B** is incorrect because unbounded history is inefficient and can crowd out important context.

**C** is incorrect because constant resets discard useful state.

**D** is incorrect because dropping constraints harms reliability.

questions.stem:
A multi-turn assistant conversation has been running for 200 turns. Each turn appends the full exchange. The system prompt is 2,000 tokens, and each turn averages 500 tokens. The team notices performance degrading after turn 100.

What is the most likely cause?

options.text: Claude becomes less attentive after 100 turns due to computational fatigue
The total context (system prompt + 200 turns × 500 tokens) approaches or exceeds the context window limit
The model's memory is corrupted after 100 turns of storage
Long conversations always degrade — this is a known API limitation

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because Claude does not experience 'computational fatigue' — each API call is stateless.

**B** is correct because 200 turns × 500 tokens = 100,000 tokens of conversation history plus the 2,000-token system prompt approaches the context window for many models. As context fills, older turns compete for attention with recent ones, degrading response quality.

**C** is incorrect because Claude has no persistent memory to corrupt — each API call processes the provided messages fresh.

**D** is incorrect because multi-turn conversations don't inherently degrade — the issue is context window utilisation, which can be managed.

[Context windows - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/context-windows)

questions.stem:
A team processes legal documents averaging 200,000 tokens each. Their model has a 200,000-token context window. They allocate the full context to the document and leave no room for instructions, few-shot examples, or the model's output.

What is the consequence of this allocation?

options.text: No consequence — Claude allocates output space automatically even without explicit room
Claude cannot process the document because input + output must always equal the context window
Claude may truncate the document, produce minimal output, or fail — because the context window must accommodate input, instructions, and output together
Claude compresses the document internally to make room for output

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because the context window is a shared budget for input tokens (system prompt + messages) and max_tokens for output. Filling it entirely with the document leaves no room.

**B** is incorrect because the context window covers input; output capacity is controlled by max_tokens, which is separate. However, if the input exhausts the window, the API will reject the request.

**C** is correct because a 200,000-token document in a 200,000-token context window leaves zero room for system prompt, user instructions, or model output. The API will likely return an error or truncate content. The team must either use a larger context model, chunk the document, or summarise it.

**D** is incorrect because Claude does not internally compress documents to create output space.

[Context windows - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/context-windows)

questions.stem:
A retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) pipeline retrieves 20 documents (3,000 tokens each = 60,000 tokens total) and places them all in the context. The user's question is about a topic covered by documents 14-16. Claude's answer is based on documents 1-3 instead.

Which context design change most directly fixes this?

options.text: Retrieve fewer documents so the total context is smaller
Place the user's question at the top of the context so Claude sees it first
Re-rank retrieved documents by relevance and place the most relevant ones nearest to the question
Use a higher temperature so Claude explores more document sources before answering

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because retrieving fewer documents reduces recall. The problem is placement, not quantity.

**B** is incorrect because placing the question first helps the model understand the goal, but the relevant documents (14-16) are still far from the question if ordering is unchanged.

**C** is correct because re-ranking places the most relevant documents (14-16) nearest to the question in context. Models attend more strongly to content close to the query. This directly counters the lost-in-the-middle effect.

**D** is incorrect because temperature increases output diversity but does not change which documents the model attends to.

[Context windows - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/context-windows)

questions.stem:
An agent must continue work across sessions and remember prior decisions about customer preferences.

Which pattern BEST supports that need?

options.text: Persist the relevant state externally and restore it when the next session begins
Rely on the model to remember past sessions automatically without stored state
Delete decision history after every response
Increase temperature so the model guesses the prior state more creatively

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because cross-session continuity requires serialized state or memory outside the model.

**B** is incorrect because session memory is not automatic across restarts.

**C** is incorrect because deleting state prevents continuity.

**D** is incorrect because randomness does not reconstruct missing memory.

questions.stem:
A conversational agent must remember a user's preferences across sessions (e.g., language preference, formatting style, project constraints). The agent does not use a database.

Which approach maintains preference continuity across sessions without a database?

options.text: Increase max_tokens so the model retains more information within a session
Ask the user to repeat their preferences at the start of every session
At the end of each session, extract and store the user's preferences as structured text that can be injected into the system prompt at the start of the next session
Claude automatically retains preferences between sessions through its built-in memory

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because max_tokens affects response length, not cross-session memory. The API is stateless — no information persists between sessions.

**B** is incorrect because requiring users to repeat preferences every session is a poor experience and defeats the purpose of a persistent assistant.

**C** is correct because extracting preferences at session end and storing them as structured text (e.g., in a file) that gets injected into the system prompt at session start is the standard cross-session continuity pattern when a database is not available.

**D** is incorrect because Claude has no built-in cross-session memory. Each API session starts fresh.

[Context windows - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/context-windows)

questions.stem:
A team uses prompt caching to reduce costs on a legal research assistant. The system prompt includes a 30,000-token legal knowledge base. Caching is enabled with `cache_control: ephemeral`.

What is the minimum cache hit requirement for caching to reduce costs compared to not caching?

options.text: At least 1 cache hit — any reuse saves money
At least 2 cache hits — the first creates the cache (at full cost), so savings only begin on the second request
At least 10 cache hits — caching has significant overhead that must be amortised
Cache hits never save money on system prompts — only tool results can be cached

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because the first request that creates the cache entry incurs the full cost plus a small cache write overhead. The first subsequent request (second total) is when savings begin.

**B** is correct because prompt caching works as follows: the first request writes the cache entry (full cost + write fee). Every subsequent request that hits the cache pays the reduced cache-read price. So savings start from the second request onward.

**C** is incorrect because the cache write overhead is small. Just two total requests (one write, one read) are sufficient for net savings on a 30,000-token prefix.

**D** is incorrect because system prompts and message prefixes are the primary targets for prompt caching.

[Prompt caching - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/prompt-caching)

questions.stem:
A team's agentic pipeline builds up context over 40 tool calls. Each tool call appends a response averaging 2,000 tokens. After 40 calls, the conversation is 80,000 tokens. The team wants to continue the task for 80 more calls without exceeding 200,000 tokens.

Which context management strategy achieves this?

options.text: Start a new conversation after every 40 tool calls and pass only the final result forward
After each 10 tool calls, summarise the last 10 tool results into 2,000 tokens and replace them in the context
Increase max_tokens on each tool call to extend the effective context
Remove all tool call results and re-run the tools when the context fills up

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because starting a new conversation discards all intermediate context, losing the accumulated task state that the pipeline needs to continue correctly.

**B** is correct because periodic summarisation compresses older tool results (replacing 10 × 2,000 = 20,000 tokens with a 2,000-token summary), allowing the pipeline to continue running within the context window budget. This is the rolling-window summarisation pattern for long-running tasks.

**C** is incorrect because max_tokens controls output length, not input context capacity.

**D** is incorrect because re-running tools wastes resources and may produce different results on second runs, breaking task continuity.

[Context windows - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/context-windows)

questions.stem:
A team wants repeatable Claude Code behavior across the repository for code review, test commands, and style rules.

Which approach is BEST?

options.text: Store the project instructions in the repository-level `CLAUDE.md` so the workflow is shared with the team
Ask each developer to remember the rules manually
Put critical workflow rules only in an ephemeral chat message
Randomize the review steps per run

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because repository-level Claude Code instructions make behavior durable and shareable.

**B** is incorrect because manual recall is inconsistent.

**C** is incorrect because ephemeral chat instructions are easy to lose.

**D** is incorrect because randomized workflows reduce repeatability.

questions.stem:
A developer finds that Claude Code frequently edits files in the `node_modules/` directory, causing unintended changes to dependencies. They want Claude Code to ignore this directory entirely.

Which Claude Code mechanism prevents it from accessing `node_modules/`?

options.text: Add `node_modules/` to the project `.gitignore` file — Claude Code respects gitignore
Create a `.claudeignore` file at the project root listing `node_modules/` to tell Claude Code to skip this directory
Add a CLAUDE.md instruction: 'Never edit files in node_modules'
Claude Code automatically ignores node_modules because it is a standard dependency directory

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because Claude Code's ignore mechanism is distinct from Git's `.gitignore`.

**B** is correct because Claude Code respects `.claudeignore` files using the same glob pattern syntax as `.gitignore`. Adding `node_modules/` to `.claudeignore` tells Claude Code to exclude that directory from file operations.

**C** is incorrect because CLAUDE.md provides guidance that Claude may follow but is not a hard exclusion mechanism.

**D** is incorrect because Claude Code does not have built-in knowledge that `node_modules/` should be excluded — this must be explicitly configured.

[Overview - Claude Code Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/overview)

questions.stem:
A team wants all Claude Code sessions in their project to use a specific set of MCP servers and allowed tools, regardless of which developer starts the session.

Which configuration makes these settings apply to every developer automatically?

options.text: Each developer configures their personal user-level settings with the MCP servers and tools
Store the MCP server list and allowed tools in the project's `.claude/settings.json` and commit it to the repository
Document the required settings in CLAUDE.md and ask developers to configure them manually
Use environment variables to pass MCP server configuration at session startup

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because user-level settings are personal and not shared across the team. Each developer would need to configure independently, and the settings could drift.

**B** is correct because `.claude/settings.json` at the project level is version-controlled and applies to every developer who clones the repository. MCP server definitions and tool restrictions committed here are automatically picked up by all team members.

**C** is incorrect because documenting in CLAUDE.md and relying on manual configuration is error-prone — not all developers will configure correctly.

**D** is incorrect because environment variables require each developer to set them up manually and are not persisted in the repository.

[Claude Code Settings](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/settings)

questions.stem:
A developer runs Claude Code in a new repository and types a question. Claude Code correctly answers about the code but suggests a pattern incompatible with the team's architecture conventions. The conventions are documented in `ARCHITECTURE.md`.

What is the most efficient way to ensure Claude Code always applies these conventions?

options.text: Paste the contents of `ARCHITECTURE.md` into every session as a user message
Add `@ARCHITECTURE.md` to the project-level CLAUDE.md so the conventions are automatically included in every session's context
Tell Claude Code 'Please read ARCHITECTURE.md' at the start of each session
Email the team to always include architecture constraints in their questions to Claude Code

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because pasting conventions manually into every session is repetitive and error-prone.

**B** is correct because the `@filename` import syntax in CLAUDE.md automatically includes the referenced file's contents in every session. Adding `@ARCHITECTURE.md` to CLAUDE.md means all sessions load the conventions without manual intervention.

**C** is incorrect because asking Claude Code to read the file at session start requires remembering to do so every time and is not automatic.

**D** is incorrect because asking team members to include architecture constraints manually is not scalable or reliable.

[Claude Code: Memory](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/memory)

questions.stem:
A document-processing pipeline has six independent analysis tasks that can each run in under two minutes. The team currently runs them sequentially, taking 12+ minutes per document. They want to reduce this to under 3 minutes.

Which architectural approach achieves this?

options.text: Increase max_tokens on the single Claude call so it can process all six tasks faster
Use a prompt chain with all six tasks in series but with shorter prompts
Run six subagents in parallel, each handling one analysis task concurrently
Cache the document in the system prompt to eliminate re-loading overhead

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because max_tokens affects output length, not processing speed. Combining six tasks in one call does not parallelize them.

**B** is incorrect because a prompt chain still runs tasks in series — reducing prompt length does not change the sequential nature.

**C** is correct because launching six subagents simultaneously allows all six analysis tasks to run concurrently. Wall-clock time drops from sum-of-all to max-of-one, achieving the sub-3-minute target.

**D** is incorrect because prompt caching reduces input token costs but does not parallelize task execution.

[Building effective agents - Anthropic](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/building-effective-agents)

questions.stem:
A team submits a Message Batches API request containing 15,000 individual model requests. The API returns a 400 error: the batch is rejected.

What is the most likely cause?

options.text: The batch contains requests using more than 10 different system prompts
The batch was submitted outside the allowed processing window for batch jobs
The total input tokens across all 15,000 requests exceeds the per-batch token limit
The Message Batches API has a per-batch limit of 10,000 requests — a batch of 15,000 exceeds this limit and is rejected

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because the Message Batches API does not impose a limit on the number of distinct system prompts in a batch. Each request is independent and can use any system prompt.

**B** is incorrect because the Message Batches API does not have a time-of-day submission window. Batches can be submitted at any time and are processed asynchronously, typically within 24 hours.

**C** is incorrect because while there are per-request token limits, the per-batch token count is not the primary constraint causing this rejection. The batch size limit is the relevant constraint here.

**D** is correct because the Message Batches API enforces a maximum of 10,000 requests per batch. Submitting 15,000 requests in a single batch exceeds this limit and results in a rejection. The correct approach is to split the 15,000 requests across two or more batches of ≤10,000 each.

[Message Batches - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/message-batches)

questions.stem:
A team runs four Claude Code subagents in parallel on the same repository. Two agents modify files in `src/`, one modifies `tests/`, and one modifies `docs/`. After all agents finish, several file conflicts appear.

Which setup would have prevented these conflicts?

options.text: Assign each agent a separate Git worktree so each has its own isolated working directory
Run the agents sequentially instead of in parallel
Give each agent a read-only clone of the repository
Configure Claude Code to lock files before editing them

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Git worktrees give each agent a separate working directory backed by the same Git repo but with independent file states. Agents in different worktrees cannot produce file conflicts with each other.

**B** is incorrect because sequential execution eliminates parallelism — the performance benefit is lost.

**C** is incorrect because read-only clones prevent agents from writing any changes, defeating the purpose of the task.

**D** is incorrect because Claude Code does not provide a built-in file-locking mechanism for multi-agent coordination.

[Overview - Claude Code Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/overview)

questions.stem:
A developer asks Claude Code: 'Add authentication to the app.' Before writing any code, Claude Code asks: 'Do you want session-based or token-based auth? Should I add OAuth providers? What database is being used?'

What pattern does this behaviour represent, and why is it appropriate here?

options.text: Unnecessary questioning — Claude Code should make sensible defaults and start coding immediately
The interview pattern — gathering requirements before starting a complex irreversible task reduces costly mid-task corrections
An error — Claude Code is failing to follow the instruction provided
Over-caution — authentication is straightforward and needs no clarification

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because 'sensible defaults' for authentication would require arbitrary choices that may be completely wrong for this codebase. The cost of picking wrong is high.

**B** is correct because the interview pattern — asking clarifying questions before beginning a complex task — is appropriate when the task has irreversible consequences and multiple valid implementations. Getting the answers upfront prevents costly mid-implementation pivots.

**C** is incorrect because asking clarifying questions is a valid and desirable behaviour, not a failure to follow instructions.

**D** is incorrect because authentication choice (session vs token, OAuth vs password, database schema) is deeply architecture-dependent and varies significantly across projects.

[Overview - Claude Code Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/overview)

questions.stem: A developer wants Claude Code to implement a new feature using test-driven development. The codebase already has a test suite. How should the developer start the session to get the best TDD workflow?

options.text: Ask Claude Code to write the feature implementation first, then add tests afterward
Provide the failing test files to Claude Code at the start, then ask it to implement the feature until all tests pass
Tell Claude Code the test framework being used and let it infer what tests to write
Run the tests first to confirm they pass, then ask Claude Code to add more tests

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because writing implementation before tests reverses the TDD cycle and does not leverage the existing test suite.

**B** is correct because providing the failing test files gives Claude Code concrete, machine-verifiable success criteria. Claude can implement code, run the tests, observe failures, revise the implementation, and iterate until all tests pass — a precise TDD loop.

**C** is incorrect because letting Claude infer tests from framework knowledge produces tests without connection to the existing codebase's actual requirements.

**D** is incorrect because tests that already pass do not drive new feature development. TDD starts with failing tests for the feature to be built.

[Claude Code GitHub Actions](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/github-actions)

questions.stem:
A team uses an internal MCP server for database access. Each developer has different credentials. The team also uses a shared read-only MCP server for documentation lookup that should be available to everyone.

Where should each MCP server be configured?

options.text: Both servers in the project `.mcp.json` — credentials are handled by environment variables
Both servers in the user-level config — all MCP servers should be personal
Database MCP in user-level config (personal credentials); documentation MCP in project `.mcp.json` (shared)
Database MCP in project `.mcp.json` with a placeholder credential; documentation MCP in user-level config

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because placing the database MCP in project config requires each developer's personal credentials to be in a shared file, which is a security risk.

**B** is incorrect because putting shared read-only servers in user-level config requires every developer to independently configure the same server, creating duplication and drift.

**C** is correct because the database MCP uses personal credentials, so it belongs in user-level config where each developer provides their own. The documentation MCP is shared and read-only, so it belongs in project `.mcp.json` where it is version-controlled and available to the whole team.

**D** is incorrect because a placeholder credential in project config does not solve the per-developer authentication problem.

[Claude Code Settings](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/settings)

questions.stem:
A team wants to prevent Claude Code from using the Bash tool to run test suites in any directory outside of `/app/tests/`. All other Bash commands should still be allowed.

Which approach achieves this with deterministic enforcement?

options.text: Add 'Only run tests in /app/tests/' to CLAUDE.md and trust Claude to follow it
Remove the Bash tool from Claude Code's allowed tools list entirely
Create a PreToolUse hook that inspects Bash commands matching test-runner patterns and blocks those run outside the allowed directory
Set the working directory to `/app/tests/` before starting Claude Code so relative paths resolve correctly

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because CLAUDE.md instructions are guidance, not enforcement. Claude may still run tests outside the directory if it judges that necessary.

**B** is incorrect because removing Bash entirely prevents Claude from running any shell command, which is far too restrictive.

**C** is correct because a PreToolUse hook inspects every Bash command before execution and can block specific patterns — such as test-runner commands invoked outside `/app/tests/` — with a non-zero exit code. This is deterministic and cannot be overridden by Claude's reasoning.

**D** is incorrect because setting the starting directory only affects the initial working directory. Claude can still change directories during execution.

[Claude Code Hooks](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/hooks)

questions.stem:
A Claude Code skill definition includes the following frontmatter:

```yaml
context: fork
allowed-tools: [Read, Grep, Glob]
```

What does `context: fork` mean for this skill's execution?

options.text: The skill creates a Git branch before executing so its changes are isolated
The skill runs in a forked process separate from the main Claude Code shell
The skill executes with a copy of the current session context rather than continuing in the same session, keeping the main session clean
The skill requires the user to confirm before it starts so they can fork the session manually if needed

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because `context: fork` is not related to Git branching — it controls session context isolation, not version control.

**B** is incorrect because `context: fork` does not create a separate OS process.

**C** is correct because `context: fork` gives the skill execution a copy of the current session context. Any changes the skill makes to the conversation context do not affect the main session, keeping the parent session clean after the skill completes.

**D** is incorrect because `context: fork` is an automatic configuration setting, not a user confirmation prompt.

[Claude Code Slash Commands](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/slash-commands)

options.text: It is a template placeholder that Claude Code will prompt the user to fill in at startup
It expands the environment variable `DB_PASSWORD` from the shell at server startup, so secrets are not stored in the config file
It instructs the MCP server to generate a new random password each time it starts
It is YAML syntax for referencing another config key named `DB_PASSWORD`

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because Claude Code does not prompt users to fill in template placeholders.

**B** is correct because `.mcp.json` supports environment variable expansion using the `${VAR_NAME}` syntax. The value of the shell environment variable is substituted at server startup, so credentials can be stored in the developer's environment (e.g., `.env` file or shell profile) without being committed to the config file.

**C** is incorrect because Claude Code does not generate random secrets from variable syntax.

**D** is incorrect because `.mcp.json` is JSON, not YAML, and the `${...}` syntax is specifically for environment variable expansion, not JSON cross-referencing.

[Claude Code Settings](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/settings)

questions.stem:
A developer wants Claude Code to never use built-in file-editing tools and instead always use a project-specific `safe_write` MCP tool that includes validation and backup logic.

Which configuration achieves this?

options.text: Set `allowed-tools: [safe_write]` in CLAUDE.md and describe why the built-in tools should be avoided
Remove the Edit and Write tools from Claude Code's built-in tool list via the settings panel
Set `denyTools: [Edit, Write]` in Claude Code settings and add `safe_write` to `allowedTools`
Rename the built-in Edit and Write tools in the MCP config so they route to safe_write

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because CLAUDE.md guidance instructs Claude but does not enforce tool restrictions — Claude may still use built-in tools if it judges them appropriate.

**B** is incorrect because built-in Claude Code tools cannot be removed via a settings panel; they are controlled through the `denyTools` and `allowedTools` configuration keys.

**C** is correct because `denyTools` explicitly blocks named tools from being used, and `allowedTools` ensures the `safe_write` MCP tool is available. Together they enforce that only `safe_write` is used for file operations.

**D** is incorrect because Claude Code MCP config does not support renaming or aliasing built-in tools.

[Claude Code: Memory](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/memory)

questions.stem:
A pipeline must extract a JSON object from unstructured text with exact field names: `invoice_number`, `total_amount`, `currency`. The team tried adding the schema to the system prompt in prose, but Claude occasionally returns fields with slightly different names.

What is the most reliable way to enforce exact field names?

options.text: List the exact field names in the system prompt with the note 'Do not deviate from these names'
Define a tool whose `input_schema` specifies all three fields as required properties, and force a tool call via `tool_choice`
Use regex post-processing to normalise field names after Claude's response
Ask Claude to double-check its own field names before responding

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because prose instructions reduce but do not eliminate variation. Claude may still occasionally deviate in complex or ambiguous contexts.

**B** is correct because a tool definition's `input_schema` is machine-validated JSON Schema. Required properties with exact names must appear in the tool call or the API rejects the response. This provides schema-level enforcement that prose instructions cannot match.

**C** is incorrect because regex normalisation is fragile and may misidentify similar-looking field names or fail on new variations.

**D** is incorrect because self-review by Claude is subject to the same failure modes as the original extraction step.

[Tool use with Claude - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/tool-use)

questions.stem:
A developer needs Claude to always call a specific tool named `log_decision` at the end of every response in a compliance pipeline. Setting `tool_choice` to `{"type": "auto"}` sometimes skips the tool call when Claude decides it isn't needed.

Which `tool_choice` setting guarantees the tool is always called?

options.text: `{"type": "any"}` — Claude must call at least one tool from the available list
`{"type": "none"}` — prevents Claude from using any other tools, leaving only `log_decision`
`{"type": "tool", "name": "log_decision"}` — forces Claude to call that specific tool
`{"type": "required"}` — marks all tools as required

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because `{"type": "any"}` forces Claude to call some tool, but it may call a different tool in the list rather than `log_decision` specifically.

**B** is incorrect because `{"type": "none"}` disables all tool calls, including `log_decision`.

**C** is correct because `{"type": "tool", "name": "log_decision"}` forces Claude to call exactly the named tool. This is the mechanism for requiring a specific tool call in every response.

**D** is incorrect because `{"type": "required"}` is not a valid tool_choice type in the Anthropic API.

[Tool use with Claude - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/tool-use)

questions.stem:
A medical coding pipeline needs Claude to classify each patient encounter as one of seven acuity levels: CRITICAL, SEVERE, HIGH, MODERATE, LOW, MINIMAL, or OBSERVATION. Across 10,000 production runs, 34 responses use labels outside this set — 'MODERATE-HIGH', 'UNCLEAR', or free-text explanations — causing downstream billing system crashes.

Which approach most directly eliminates out-of-set labels?

options.text: Increase max_tokens to give Claude more space to reason about the correct acuity level
Set temperature to 0.8 to allow more flexible label generation
Remove the label list from the prompt so Claude generates its own consistent label set organically

Define the seven permitted labels explicitly in the system prompt with brief criteria for each, include an example of the correct single-label output format, and add a validation-retry loop that rejects any response not matching the permitted set

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because `max_tokens` controls response length, not output accuracy or label conformance. A longer response does not constrain Claude to the permitted label set.

**B** is incorrect because higher temperature increases stochastic variation in token selection — it makes Claude more likely to produce creative or non-standard outputs, worsening out-of-set label rates.

**C** is incorrect because removing the label list eliminates the only constraint that defines what constitutes a valid output. Without an explicit label set, Claude will produce whatever label seems most natural — which is exactly the behaviour causing the crashes.

**D** is correct because the increase-consistency pattern for predefined outputs requires three coordinated elements: an explicit list of permitted values in the system prompt (narrows the output space), an example of the correct output format (removes ambiguity about how to express the label), and a programmatic validation-retry loop (catches non-conforming responses before they reach downstream systems). Together these eliminate the conditions that produce out-of-set labels.

[Increase consistency - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/test-and-evaluate/strengthen-guardrails/increase-consistency)

questions.stem:
A legal analysis pipeline asks Claude to review contracts for missing standard clauses, ambiguous liability language, and regulatory compliance gaps — all in a single prompt. Review accuracy is 78%. The team wants to reach 95%+.

Which prompt engineering approach most directly improves accuracy on multi-criterion review tasks?

options.text: Expand the single prompt with more detailed descriptions of all three criteria

Run three separate Claude calls — one per criterion — each with a focused prompt for that single criterion; each focused call can apply full attention to one dimension without competing with the others
Increase temperature so Claude explores the contract more creatively across all criteria
Shorten the prompt to reduce Claude's cognitive load across all three criteria simultaneously

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because expanding the single prompt adds more text but does not reduce the cognitive competition between criteria. Claude must still track three distinct review dimensions simultaneously, which dilutes attention on each.

**B** is correct because decomposing a multi-criterion task into separate focused prompts is a fundamental prompt engineering technique. When each call covers only one criterion, Claude can apply its full attention — context window, reasoning capacity, and pattern matching — to that dimension without competing requirements. This consistently produces higher per-criterion accuracy than a single all-in-one prompt.

**C** is incorrect because higher temperature increases output randomness. For a precise review task with specific criteria, randomness worsens accuracy rather than improving it.

**D** is incorrect because shortening the prompt removes the criteria detail Claude needs to perform the review correctly. Less information leads to less accurate, not more accurate, analysis.

[Prompt engineering overview - Claude API Docs](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/build-with-claude/prompt-engineering/overview)

questions.stem:
A team is using the Message Batches API to process 8,000 independent customer queries overnight. After submitting the batch, a developer asks: 'Can we ask follow-up questions in the same batch if the initial answers need clarification?'

What is the correct answer?

options.text: Yes — the batch can be updated with follow-up turns before the 24-hour window closes
Yes — each request in the batch supports up to 10 conversation turns
No — the Message Batches API processes single request-response pairs; multi-turn conversations require separate synchronous API calls
No — batches are processed FIFO so follow-up questions must wait for the entire batch to complete

questions.explanation:
**A** is incorrect because submitted batches cannot be updated with additional turns — each request is an independent unit.

**B** is incorrect because the Message Batches API does not support multi-turn conversations within a single request. Each request maps to exactly one response.

**C** is correct because the Message Batches API is designed for high-volume independent request-response pairs, not multi-turn dialogues. If follow-up conversation is needed based on batch results, those follow-ups must be made as separate synchronous API calls after batch results are retrieved.

**D** is incorrect because FIFO ordering does not apply; batches process items asynchronously and independently.
